>The oil rally due to the war in Iran will not save the Russian budget

>Because of the war in Iran, Brent oil prices have reached $83 per barrel, but this will not help Russia balance its budget, bloated by the costs of the war in Ukraine.

>In order for its deficit to reach the planned 3.8 trillion rubles, the average annual export price of Russian Urals oil should be $59 per barrel - the budget is based on this. And for budget revenues to equal expenses, $97 is required, Alfa Bank analysts estimated.

>In January, the average price of Urals was $41. The Ministry of Economic Development has not yet summed up the results of February. At the same time, the price of the benchmark Brent spent most of the month in the $60-70 corridor. Due to sanctions, discounts on Russian oil jumped at the end of last year and have not decreased since then: in February they were $27-28.

>The oil rally had almost no impact on Urals. On March 2 it cost $46.4, notes the chief economist of Renaissance Capital Andrey Melashchenko. Such Urals quotes “imply that the discount further widened in early March,” he states.

>In addition to the huge discounts that Russia is forced to give to oil buyers, budget revenues are being reduced by the strong ruble. The budget was drawn up based on the average annual rate of 92,2 rubles/$, but so far the dollar is cheaper than 80 rubles. As a result, the ruble price of a barrel, which forms oil and gas budget revenues, was less than 3,500 rubles in February. - a third less than the planned 5440 on average for the year. The preliminary estimated ruble tax price of Urals on March 2 was 3,582 rubles. (Urals – $46.4 per barrel; dollar exchange rate – 77,3 rubles), Melashchenko estimates.

The problem is still the total number.
There aren't enough missiles.
But there are roughly 3500 Tomahawks in total inventory (minus 400 in the middle east after the first salvos), and <1500 air launched cruise missiles (with sufficient range to be relevant, JASSM can become relevant when AD is sufficiently degraded, but that is not the case now).

And yearly production rate of 90-100.

This is a lot, but remember that almost all of those missiles are already locked in VLS tubes on ships and submarines.
I mean, you had an inventory before Feb 28th of 3500 TLAM carried in primarily 14 Ohio class subs (capacity 154 Tomahawks each! So 2156 total) and 74 Arleigh Burke class ships (theoretical capacity 96 each or 7104 total)

Assuming subs have 1000, and Arleigh Burkes have about 30 each, that gives a theoretical replenishment of 280 missiles unless they can replace the deployed ships by moving them from another fleet.
400 missiles in this example would be the full load of 10 Arleigh Burkes (and it was 9 + 1 Ticonderoga or something) + about 70% of the missiles on two escorting Ohio class submarines.

>>529833930
Thats just paints with varying emissivity levels. These paints can make a surface emit various levels of infrared radiation. For instance, high-emissivity paints (like those mimicking hot engine parts or exhaust) can make sections appear warmer (brighter in IR) while low-emissivity materials (similar to polished metals) reflect ambient radiation and appear cooler, even if the entire structure is at the same physical temperature as the ground. This creates a false thermal profile that mimics a real helicopter.

replacing $1.7B of equipment is roughly:
0.2% of the annual U.S. defense budget
~0.005% of U.S. GDP
Financially, that is trivial for the U.S.
But that does NOT mean “infinite replacements.”
There are three real limits:
Production speed
Some systems (like early-warning radars or missile interceptors) take years to manufacture, even if the money exists.
Strategic gaps
Destroying a radar or sensor may create a temporary blind spot in missile defense even if it is later replaced.
Escalation risk
The bigger damage in wars between major states usually comes from missiles, ships, aircraft, infrastructure, and logistics, not single radar sites.
Reality check
Countries like Iran generally cannot win by economic attrition against the U.S. economy alone. The U.S. economy is the largest in the world, giving it enormous ability to absorb losses. �
Wikipedia
But wars are rarely decided purely by money — they are decided by time, logistics, politics, alliances, and escalation.
Bottom line
$1.7B loss financially trivial for the U.S.
Does not mean Iran cannot cause real military problems
Replacement is limited more by time and production, not money.

comment:
2 × AN/GSC-52B terminals (Bahrain) – $16.8M each = $33.6M total. Estimate combines hardware cost and part of the site infrastructure required for large military satellite communication earth terminals. Budget data suggests about $7M procurement for two terminals and ~$9.6M for the supporting site (electronics building, power and infrastructure). Because these terminals require dedicated support facilities, a practical replacement estimate is about $16.8M per terminal including its share of site support.

3 × radomes (Arifjan base, Kuwait) – $7.0M each = $20.9M total. Military radomes are specialized protective structures for radar or communications antennas. Contract listings show radomes around $6.9M, so ~$7M is a reasonable rounded replacement estimate including installation.

1 × AN/TPY-2 radar (UAE) – $243.27M. Missile Defense Agency procurement documents list one AN/TPY-2 radar at roughly this cost.

1 × AN/TPY-2 radar (Jordan) – $243.27M. Same system and same procurement estimate.

1 × AN/FPS-132 radar (Qatar) – $1.10B. DSCA notifications list the AN/FPS-132 Block 5 early-warning radar package at about $1.1B including equipment and integration.

8 × satcom buildings/structures (Kuwait) – $5.0M each = $40.0M total. Secure communications support buildings with power, cooling and technical infrastructure commonly fall in the several-million range; $5M is a reasonable middle estimate.

1 × site near radome possible AN/TPY-2-related hit (Prince Sultan AB, Saudi Arabia) – $25.0M. Treated as partial site damage rather than a full radar loss.

TOTAL ≈ $1.71B

>>529838196
replacing $1.7B of equipment is roughly:
0.2% of the annual U.S. defense budget
~0.005% of U.S. GDP
Financially, that is trivial for the U.S.
But that does NOT mean “infinite replacements.”
There are three real limits:
Production speed
Some systems (like early-warning radars or missile interceptors) take years to manufacture, even if the money exists.
Strategic gaps
Destroying a radar or sensor may create a temporary blind spot in missile defense even if it is later replaced.
Escalation risk
The bigger damage in wars between major states usually comes from missiles, ships, aircraft, infrastructure, and logistics, not single radar sites.
Reality check
Countries like Iran generally cannot win by economic attrition against the U.S. economy alone. The U.S. economy is the largest in the world, giving it enormous ability to absorb losses. �
Wikipedia
But wars are rarely decided purely by money — they are decided by time, logistics, politics, alliances, and escalation.
Bottom line
$1.7B loss financially trivial for the U.S.
Does not mean Iran cannot cause real military problems
Replacement is limited more by time and production, not money.

There are several major interpretive approaches (often called hermeneutical methods) used to understand the Book of Revelation in the New Testament. Most Christian interpretations fall into four primary schools:

1. Preterist

Core idea: Most (or all) of Revelation was fulfilled in the 1st century.

Written to address persecution under the Nero or Domitian.

The “Beast” is often identified with Nero or the Roman Empire.

The fall of “Babylon” is usually seen as the destruction of Jerusalem (70 AD) or pagan Rome.

Strength: Takes the original audience seriously.
Weakness: Can seem to downplay future prophecy.

There are:

Partial preterists (some prophecies still future)

Full preterists (all fulfilled — controversial in orthodox Christianity)

2. Historicist

Core idea: Revelation outlines the course of church history from the apostolic age to the end of the world.

Popular during the Reformation.

The Beast was often identified with the Papacy by Reformers like Martin Luther.

Events correspond to major historical developments in Europe.

Strength: Connects prophecy to long-term historical patterns.
Weakness: Highly subjective; interpretations change as history progresses.
subject: FREEMASONS AND JEWS ARE TRYING TO INITIATE THE END TIMES *NOT A SCHIZO POST*

name: Anonymous
comment:
>>529816256
3. Futurist

Core idea: Most of Revelation (chapters 4–22) describes future events still to occur.

Common in modern evangelicalism.

Associated with dispensationalism and premillennialism.

The Antichrist, Tribulation, and a literal 1,000-year reign are future.

Often linked to figures like:

John Nelson Darby

Tim LaHaye

Strength: Preserves a strong future prophetic dimension.
Weakness: Sometimes criticized for reading modern geopolitics into the text.

4. Idealist (Symbolic / Spiritual)

Core idea: Revelation is not tied to specific historical events but depicts the timeless struggle between good and evil.

The Beast represents oppressive political power in every age.

Babylon represents corrupt world systems.

Focus is theological rather than predictive.

Influential among theologians like:

Augustine of Hippo (in broader eschatology)

Strength: Avoids date-setting and speculation.
Weakness: Can seem to dissolve specific historical meaning.

Additional Layer: Millennial Views

Interpretation also overlaps with views of the “thousand years” in Revelation 20:

Premillennialism – Christ returns before a literal millennium.

Amillennialism – Millennium is symbolic (common in Catholic and Reformed traditions).

Postmillennialism – Christ returns after a golden age of Christian influence.

Academic-Critical Method

Modern biblical scholarship also analyzes Revelation using:

Literary criticism (apocalyptic genre)

Intertextual study (heavy use of Book of Daniel and Old Testament imagery)

Historical context (Roman imperial cult, persecution)

Greco-Roman symbolism

Seven British members of a global child pornography ring named Wonderland face prison after pleading guilty to their roles in the distribution of 750,000 obscene pictures on the internet.

They were among more than 100 men arrested in 107 coordinated raids across three continents in the largest international police operation mounted against any crime.

Police forces around the world have been asked to trace the child victims, dozens of whom were under five years old. So far, only three have been found.

US authorities have named the man who spawned Wonderland: Peter Giordano, 37, a sales clerk for an electronics supply store, who was arrested by US customs agents at his home in New York, and has since pleaded guilty to distributing child pornography.

comment: On 29 February 2020, the US, represented by diplomatic envoy Zalmay Khalilzad, and the Taliban signed the Agreement for Bringing Peace to Afghanistan, commonly known as the US–Taliban deal,[52][53] that provided for the withdrawal from Afghanistan of "all military forces of the United States, its allies, and Coalition partners, including all non-diplomatic civilian personnel, private security contractors, trainers, advisors, and supporting services personnel" within 14 months (i.e., by 1 May 2021). At the time, there were about 13,000 US troops in the country. The withdrawal was conditional on the Taliban upholding the terms of the agreement that included "not to allow al-Qaeda or any other extremist group to operate in the areas they control".[54] The US was to reduce its forces in Afghanistan by about 5,000 troops to 8,600 within 135 days.[55][56][57][58][52][59] NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg pledged to initially reduce NATO's numbers from roughly 16,000 troops to about 12,000.[60]
